---
title: "Week 3 — Facilitator Notes"
subtitle: "Data Structures & Tidy Data"
---

## Learning Objectives

Residents should understand how R stores data (vectors, data frames, lists) and why tidy data structure matters for clinical analysis. This is the first week where residents write code that manipulates actual data — the transition from "document structure" (Week 2) to "data structure."

---

## Session Format

| Segment | Duration | Activity |
|---------|----------|----------|
| Recap | 5 min | Review Week 2 Quarto documents; check GitHub pushes |
| Lecture | 20 min | Data structures; vectors → data frames; clinical analogy |
| Live Demo | 20 min | Build messy → tidy dataset together; show why tidy wins |
| Independent Exercise | 30 min | Parts 1–4: residents create, inspect, tidy data |
| Group Share | 10 min | Part 4: compare messy → tidy descriptions |
| Reflection | 10 min | Part 5: write and submit |
| Check-in | 5 min | Preview Week 4 (clinical data types); verify GitHub push |

---

## Key Teaching Points

### The Chart Analogy

Frame the entire session around a clinical object residents know: the ICU flowsheet or anesthesia record.

- A **vector** = one column of the flowsheet (all heart rates)
- A **data frame** = the entire flowsheet at one timepoint
- A **list** = the patient chart with sections
- **Tidy data** = the flowsheet restructured so every row is one measurement at one time

This grounds abstract CS concepts in daily clinical experience.

### The "Mean of All HRs" Trap

The exercise intentionally makes Part 2 (messy data) difficult. The goal is frustration. When residents struggle to compute `mean()` across `hr_t1` and `hr_t2`, the teachable moment arrives:

> "The structure of your data determines the difficulty of your analysis. Bad structure makes simple questions complex."

Do not rescue them early. Let them attempt `mean(c(vitals_messy$hr_t1, vitals_messy$hr_t2))` and then ask: "What if there were 20 timepoints? 100?"

### Tidy Data as Prerequisite, Not Preference

Some residents will say "but the wide format is more compact." Acknowledge this, then push back:

> "Compact for your eyes, yes. But the computer does not read the way you do. The computer reads one cell at a time. Wide format forces you to write custom code for every question. Tidy format lets you reuse the same code for every question."

This is the core argument of the week. It justifies every subsequent analysis week.

### Base R vs. Tidyverse

This module uses **base R** (`data.frame`, `read.csv`, `mean`, `max`) intentionally. Residents are not yet ready for the tidyverse pipe syntax (`|>`) or functions (`pivot_longer`). Those come in Week 5 (EDA I) after they are comfortable with data frame basics.

The manual tidy transformation in Part 3 is tedious. That's the point. When they learn `pivot_longer()` later, the relief will be memorable and the conceptual foundation will be solid.

### The Missing CSV File

The exercise references `datasets/basic/messy-chart.csv`, which does not yet exist (dataset curation is scheduled for later). Provide two paths:

1. **If file exists:** Residents load it directly.
2. **If file does not exist:** Residents create the CSV from the provided template in Part 4.

Both paths reach the same learning goal. The second path also reinforces file I/O skills from Intro Week 4.

---

## Live Demo Script

**Build a messy dataset together:**

```r
# Start with what residents know: a patient table
patients_wide <- data.frame(
  id = c(101, 102),
  sbp_t1 = c(120, 135),
  sbp_t2 = c(118, 132),
  dbp_t1 = c(80, 85),
  dbp_t2 = c(78, 82)
)
```

**Ask the room:** "How do I get the mean of all SBP readings?" (Both patients, both timepoints.)

Let someone attempt. Usually: `mean(c(patients_wide$sbp_t1, patients_wide$sbp_t2))`. Ask: "What about 10 timepoints? What if we add MAP?"

**Build the tidy version:**

```r
patients_long <- data.frame(
  id = c(101, 101, 102, 102),
  time = c(1, 2, 1, 2),
  sbp = c(120, 118, 135, 132),
  dbp = c(80, 78, 85, 82)
)

mean(patients_long$sbp)  # 121.25 — one line, any number of timepoints
```

**Key pause:** After the first `mean()`, ask: "What changed? Not the data — the structure."

---

## Exercise Guidance

**Part 1:** Straightforward. Verify every resident can create a data frame and use `$`. Common failure: forgetting commas between vector elements.

**Part 2:** The frustration point. Circulate and watch for the "mean of all HRs" struggle. Do not solve it for them. Ask: "Can you write a single command that works for 100 timepoints?"

**Part 3:** Manual tidy creation is tedious. Some residents will try to automate with loops or `rep()`. Praise the instinct, then say: "We'll learn the automatic way next week. For now, manual builds intuition."

**Part 4:** The CSV creation step tests whether residents can write a file, then load it, then analyze it. If the file already exists, this becomes a simple `read.csv` exercise. Either path is valid.

**Part 5:** Reflection quality check. Look for:
- Recognition that EMR exports are designed for display, not analysis
- Understanding that tidy data is a prerequisite for correct statistics
- Connection to Week 1: bad structure → irreproducible analysis

---

## Troubleshooting During Session

| Problem | Quick Fix |
|---------|-----------|
| "Object not found" | Check spelling; check that the chunk with the object was executed |
| `mean()` returns `NA` | Missing values in data; add `na.rm = TRUE` |
| CSV won't load | Check file path with `getwd()`; check that file was saved |
| "Unexpected ','" in `data.frame()` | Missing closing parenthesis on previous line |
| Column names have spaces from CSV | Use backticks: `patients$`sbp preop`` or rename |
| Tidy version has wrong number of rows | Double-check that every original cell has one row in tidy version |

---

## Assessment

Verify each resident can:

- [ ] Create a data frame with multiple columns
- [ ] Access columns with `$` and compute statistics
- [ ] Explain the three tidy data rules verbally
- [ ] Identify violations in a given messy dataset
- [ ] Create or describe the tidy equivalent
- [ ] Push their Quarto document to GitHub

If not, schedule 1-on-1. Data structures are foundational — Week 4 (data types) and Week 5 (EDA) depend on this.

---

## Preparation Checklist

- [ ] Prepared messy → tidy demo on classroom machine
- [ ] Verified `datasets/basic/messy-chart.csv` exists or printed CSV template for Part 4
- [ ] Prepared "mean of all HRs" trap as teachable moment
- [ ] Identified residents who struggled with R syntax in Intro Week 4
- [ ] Tested base R commands on classroom R version

---

## Connection to Week 4

Preview: Week 4 covers clinical data types (continuous, ordinal, nominal, time-to-event). The tidy dataset you built this week will be classified by type next week. Same data, new lens.

End session with: "This week you learned how to structure data. Next week you learn how to classify it. Then you can analyze it."
